speaker: interviewer
text: Henry Oertelt, November the 21st, 1995, Saint Paul, Minnesota, USA. It's now May of 1943. And you've been continuously working at slave labor. You're continuously being harassed. Things are getting worse. What happens in May?

speaker: interviewee
text: In May of 1933-- what was it-- Two or three months after the announcement-- We are being awakened at 2 o'clock in the morning by gun butts against our door. We are opening the door, and we are looking into the faces of two black-uniformed SS men, their dog between them, gun over their shoulder. And one guy looks at his watch. And he says, you've got five-- 15 minutes. One suitcase per person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, in a way, we had resigned to the fact that probably nothing-- because during those previous two months, we didn't hear of any transports going. And so we figured that this is it. We just have to hang in here now and wait it out. And so we were really not too much prepared. And again, of course, as I said before, we figured everything has happened to the others. Why should anything happen to us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so of course we are chasing around, packing the suitcases. And after 15 minutes were up, the guy looks at his watch again. That's it. Now let's go down. We went down to the waiting truck, where they were already covered. Other families were on that had been picked up before us. And after another couple of stops, it started to get bright outside. We were then take-- we were then unloaded. After a couple of stops where another couple of families were picked up, we then were unloaded at what you would consider a collection center.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe, if you can, your feelings at that time. I mean, here you're leaving your house. And you've got 15 minutes, you know, out of the clear blue, you know, to, to leave your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's, it's a feeling of total devastation. There is no question about that. You leave all-- so many of your wonderful things behind because you have only one suitcase. You suddenly recognize that one suitcase is absolutely nothing. And so you try to decide within 15 minutes-- you knew by the time, when this guy announced 15 minutes, they mean 15 minutes. You couldn't go and say, hey, listen. I need another five minutes, mister. Would you be so nice? And so you had, you had to-- so it's, it's, it's a very devastating moment. And you throw things in. And you realize, of course, that you are being taken to a concentration camp. And you are not putting any fancy stuff in your suitcases, really the warm stuff-- whatever there-- whatever you have, solid stuff. And so it's, it's, it's a very devastating feeling. And so then we were now dropped off at the [INAUDIBLE] so-called collection center. And we were there for about three or four days or two, three days about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where this collection center was or what was called specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was called-- it, it, it was, at one time, an old-age home. And-- and it's called the Grosse Hamburger Strasse. It is still-- this building happens to be still there. I saw it just about a year ago when I was visiting Berlin again. And it is now a school. It's a Jewish school there now. And so we were there kept. And then eventually, as there were enough people collected-- new people came in, again, from this leftover 10%. There were a lot of them left. We then were taken, taken to the train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. While you were there at this collecting point, I mean, was there a lot of pandemonium going on during the day? Or did-- were you talking to other people? Other people talking to you about what was going on here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we had conversations, of course, with other people. And everybody was certainly concerned. Concerned is said mildly. Everybody was extremely upset about it, of course. But there was no pandemonium going on. Everybody was controlling themselves and so on and so forth. And besides, by that time, you know, there was SS hopping around every once in a while there, too. And if you would show too much pandemonium, they would punish you. They would take you out separately. And some people have been known to have gotten shot that way and so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see it. No, I didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, food there was pretty much normal, simple food in that collection center.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know anymore. I don't know the number, but a great, great number of people, good number of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, like thousands would you say? Or hundred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, probably by that time maybe 1,000 or so. It was a big building. And the rooms were loaded up with people. Not everybody stayed overnight in his own room. They were, we were laid on the, on the, on the floor like sardines, you know, that as it was later on in  the camps, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, during the day, were you with people, your-- you know, friends your own age or acquaintances that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There were some people that we knew, yes. Yeah, because it was all Jewish people there. And you knew a good number of them, although not all of them. Berlin was a total population of 4.5 million people. It had about a population of 175,000 Jews at the time. And so, I mean, if you knew 100 Jewish people, then that's pretty good, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened after the four days that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we the four-- I should actually-- I forgot to point out something. And allow me to go back for a moment. By that time-- when I said the-- the 90% that had disappeared-- we believed it very, very easily. Because by that time, all our family had disappeared, all my friendship group, all of them. The, the-- as I say, about eight guys most of the time had disappeared. Not one of them were left anymore. So they all, they all were gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we believed that it was. And we saw the homes of these people when we went by. They had seals sticking over their doors, over their, over the keyholes, with the official seals that were stuck on that said, this property has been confiscated. Stay away and so on and so forth. So these-- this were the circumstances. By that time, really you had lost all your friends and family members, and, you know, here and there a person or a family you had that was still there. So anyways, now here we are. And some of them we saw, also, in this collection center that I said. So as I said, eventually then, we were taken to a train station. And we were loaded up into trains. And we were on our way what turned out to be my-- I should say our, my family's-- first concentration camp, which happened to be a camp located in Czechoslovakia. The, the Germans called it Theresienstadt, or Terezin as the Czechs called it. And were you taken there on, on a regular train? Or was it the cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was an overloaded regular train. Some people didn't even find a, find a, find a seat on the benches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that you were going to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we didn't know where we were going. We didn't. It was not announced. So this-- when we arrived in Theresienstadt, of course, first of all, everything was checked out. The-- I I wanted to interject for a moment again. I said this, this was located in Czechoslovakia. By that time, Hitler had conquered a big part of Europe. And wherever he went-- when he annexed, when he annexed Austria, which he claimed all German country-- part of part of old Germany and part of Czechoslovakia-- wherever he went, he would create concentration camps. When he went into Poland, he would create these camps that, that eventually became extermination camps. Lithuania and wherever he went, he built concentration camps for a couple of reasons, I guess. One of them, it was far away. The others were far away from the German population. Although by that time, there are literally dozens of camps on German soil. But then-- and then also, of course, the eastern part of Europe had a great population of Jews. So I suppose the transportation would be a little bit simpler to get 'em from the outlying areas right in there. But this here was now Czechoslovakia. And this camp is still considered in the current annals of concentration camps as one, quote unquote, 'of the milder concentration camps.' I'd like to give you a couple of examples of a 'mild' concentration camps. First of all, to talk about children-- it is known-- documented, in fact-- that during its existence of less than-- this camp's existence of less than three years, among all the people that were sent to this camp were approximately 15,000 children. It is also known that less than 100 children in these less than three years survived, less than 100 children. There were various reasons to it. One of them, of course, was the transportations away from that camp to other camps, mostly Auschwitz, which there were also children involved. But most of them died because of the circumstances of that camp. One of the main reasons is the, the daily, quote unquote, 'menu.' Maybe it's a good idea if I, if I, if I describe that menu to you-- which, by the way, turned out the same in most of the-- in all the other camps. I was in a total of five different camps. It seems that they had all the same chief cook all over.

speaker: interviewee
text: It started out in the morning with a bowl of black water they called coffee, at noon, a bowl of watery soup with a piece of bread. Every once in awhile in that soup, you would find a bug floating around. And so, of course, when than that happened, we would dump it until we-- other prisoners that were longer in that camp and skinny would say, hey,  are you guys nuts? You know, you have-- if you don't want it, why don't you give it to us. And they came with their bowl.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't happen every time. But-- so when it happened, and eventually we gave it to them. Until finally, we started to get skinnier and skinnier. And we decided-- my brother and I were together, and our mother was in a different section-- we decided that we better take in the few calories that we can. And so, if there was a bug or something, we gingerly fished it out. And without trying to having your mind on it, you just slurped it down. Because you needed whatever calorie you could get your, get your hand on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me-- back to when you first got to the camp, what was it like when you got off the train? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like to finish the story about the children first--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I'm in the middle of it, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remind me of it. I'll be glad-- we'll come back to it. So now I'm describing the menu, which is greatly the cause of the death of children, as I'm trying to come out. And the point is here, the soup and the piece of bread was the same thing in the evening. This was basically our, the menu that every person in the camp received. I didn't talk about milk. I didn't talk about fruit or vegetable, the reason for it being that it wasn't there. Nobody got it. So the children-- what I described from the black water until the end, that's what the children received. Sundays, we got a piece of margarine and a little bag of sugar sometimes, little, tiny-- like a a restaurant size and stuff like that. But now, as you-- talking about the children-- as you can imagine, it was nothing to keep small children alive. In fact, many, many, many women were delivered to the camp pregnant. In other words, the fact that on-- that when the Nazis had the order to clear out Seventh Street east from this block to that block, they would pick up everybody that was there, whether the people were-- could walk, whether they would have to carry it on a-- carry out on stretchers, old folks, young folks, babies, women that were pregnant. Didn't make any difference. They all went onto that truck and were sent to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, therefore, a woman that arrived at that camp and would be able to bear her child very quick was probably able, for a little while, to, to feed the baby. But, but a woman that was, with this kind of a ration, in there for a little while longer, by no means when she finally bore the child was able to feed the baby. So what what I'm trying to say is I saw some women with a newborn child sitting there on the side there after we received the food-- this so-called food-- taking a piece of bread, dunking it into the watery soup, and trying to feed that to that little baby. And, of course, you can imagine the success of that. So, therefore, children didn't last very long, particularly very small children-- infants and very small ones. And so, therefore, that's part of that rate in the 'milder concentration camp' that I'm talking about. So therefore, out of 15,000, less than 100 survived, partially-- great-- greatly partially to that kind of a system. Well, now back to when you first got to Theresienstadt. Were you-- you were separated from your mother. Did you ever see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was in a separate part. But every once in a while, we were able to see her. My brother and I, fortunately, were able to stay together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, first of all, when we arrived in the camp, we looked around us. And we noticed that most people have, and all over their exposed skin, blotches. And some of them had severe sores from scratches, open that were dried and, and, and very severe-looking sores, scratches. We were wondering, at first, what that was all about, what that came from. A lot of people had it. It looked like almost everyone. Well, it didn't take too long. We found out what that-- how that was coming about. Because, pretty soon, we ourselves didn't know what we had more on our bodies, whether it was lice, fleas, or bed bugs. All these things tried to feast on your body. And you had this all over your body. And just imagine, like some people who have one lousy mosquito bite and they can't resist. Imagine you have this all over your body, and particularly children. Again, talking about children, they were much more vulnerable to this than even an, even an-- an adult. And so this was the first thing that we noticed. And then, of course, soon we were, we were assigned to workplaces. It was, at first, outside the camp, digging ditches for new drainage, for new water coming in, new pipes laying and stuff like that. In fact, with this food that I described, we were very, very much looking for other food. At one time, for a stretch of a couple or three days, we had to dig these ditches in the middle through a tomato field where they were-- tomatoes were grown. They were already ripe on the vine. And, of course, once in a while when we, when we when we had to make sure that the guard didn't look, we would pick them and would quickly swallow 'em. And, and then also now we were trying to get-- this would be wonderful to get it into the camp somehow for our wives. My brother was married then to his wife, of course. And, and not wives, our mother-- in, in case, our mother. And we tried to figure out how to smuggle them in. Because we were always frisked when we came through the gate into the camp. They came down with their hands. But we noticed that they do not really go so much between our legs. And so, as funny as-- terrible as this sounds, we figured that we'd pick the smaller tomatoes. And we had some kind of rag with a couple of safety pins where we'd maybe probably put four or five small tomatoes or more-- I don't know anymore the number-- made a roll out of it and rolled them up in this rag, put the safety pins on the end, and put them between our legs, tied them onto the pants inside. And we walked there-- a little gingerly, of course-- through the camp. Now, you see, when you are starving, and, and you have nothing of that kind ever in front of you, you don't really care much, you know. And so, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do what you had to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you do what you had to do. And this is how we smuggled some of the tomatoes in. And it was a wonderful thing. .

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about some of the people that you were with in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at, at the moment, I didn't know anybody there. So there were people of all--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any acts of brutality?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any acts of brutality that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- oh yeah. Sometimes you could see, when you walked down the street and somebody was not observant when an SS man came by and you didn't make-- get off the sidewalk or something like that. Although, you couldn't differentiate much between sidewalk and street because the streets were full with milling people, mostly old folks. The younger folks were sent to work, except maybe sometimes in late evening before curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you wouldn't watch out when you didn't get out of the way for an SS man or didn't greet him properly, like this or whatever, he would, he would start beating up whoever was around him. I've seen it a couple of times. This became, this became a scene which you didn't even register anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I started-- we worked. And I started soon to let it be known that I know how to design and build furniture. And I was then commandeered into a workshop, where I was ordered to design and to build furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say to design and build furniture, for who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most-- I never saw who it was for. But it was most likely for the commander, who found out there was some little Jew that is a craftsmen. And he wanted to take advantage of it because there were not many craftsmen around. And so I tried to design things. And I, I made the designs that I turned in purposely kind of weird so that I know-- I knew that they would say, no, that's not the way we want it. And so then I made another one until, finally, it came out that, that-- I, I figured I didn't push it too long. Because, you see, I figured to hang on with this as much as I could. Pushing a pencil does not take as much of  a calorie of the few calories that I need. So these calories, as few as they were, would last me a little bit longer-- another reason, as I said, one of the reasons that I am survive-- surviving-- one of the reasons, there is not one by itself that does it. But-- so therefore, I was trying to hold on to the pencil pushing as long as possible, rather than handling the lumber, and the wood, and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did make some furniture while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you tools and stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Tools and machinery-- everything I wanted was there. They asked me if I know how to, they asked me if I know how to carve. I had never done any carving in my life. But I said oh, yeah, sure. That's my specialty.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, I am, I am one of those guys that say, you can do anything you want to, if you want to. All right? Now, handy I was. And so if I had to do some carving, I knew I could do it. And so I did. Because they wanted to have lion claws on this, on this big conference desk that I was supposed to build out of solid mahogany, hand polished. So what, what kind of furniture did you build?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was one of the things, a huge desk. And then I was-- if I would be done with that, there were other things that they would come up with. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you receive any extra rations or anything for doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no-- not for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The-- tell you what-- this kind of-- that I could do this kind of work rather than working in stone quarries or digging ditches 10, 12 hours was much, much-- was as much worth as extra food, because I could-- in effect, it was like extra food. Because, as I say, I could space the use of calories a little bit that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You conserve your strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother kept working on for a while in those, in the field like this. But then, eventually, I was able to get him to work with me in the workshop. And that worked out fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what else did you do in Theresienstadt when you weren't working? There was a choir that was formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Theresienstadt actually was one of the-- not one of the, was the-- artist concentration camp, was all the camps that were in existence. Hitler had earmarked this camp to show the world-- when he, when he knew that people found out that Jews were picked up and sent to concentration camps-- to show the world what a wonderful life these Jews have. Yes, while they are losing their freedom, but they have freedom within that compound to do most wonderful things that even the German people and, and their German guards couldn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for instance, therefore, he sent and picked most of the famous artists, Jewish famous artists-- composers, poets, conductors, musicians, singers-- were all picked and sent to this camp. This camp, I think, had a collection in that small space of most of these geniuses that ever assembled anywhere in this world. And so-- Did you happen to see any of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course. Yeah, steadily, sure. I saw them all. And, and also rabbis, as the clergy-- he sent the famous clergy-- were specifically sent there. He had planned to show the world-- and I quote unquote, that's what he said-- 'the town that Hitler gave to the Jews.' And so what I didn't know at the time-- I might just as well first say-- that, that many of these things were secretly filmed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these artists were not only allowed, they were urged to take advantage of the allowances that, that, that we were-- that, that they can assemble and perform their arts. So my brother and I soon joined a huge choir that was in process, in progress of practicing for the, for, for, for the oratoria, oratory Handel's 'Creation,' which was performed twice eventually. And then, then the next performance, we, we started to rehearse for Mendelssohn's 'Elijah.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is a very, very odd thing. Mendelssohn, according to the racial laws of Nuremberg, was a Jew. And any where else on German-controlled soil, nothing was allowed to be performed that was written or created by Jews. And now here, he allowed us to, the commander allowed us to perform 'Elijah,' which couldn't be performed any where else on German soil. Now Mendelssohn, even though he was eventually-- had converted to Catholicism, but based on the Nuremberg laws, he was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was not a religious matter. It was a racial matter. So that's why sometimes in schools when I'm talking-- or universities-- some people ask now, if you would have converted to Christianity, do you think you could have avoided all this? No, it wouldn't have mattered. And I tell people that, even if Jesus Christ would have come to earth in Germany, he would have been sent to a concentration camp. That's, of course, why Hitler took so much pain in trying to tell people that Hitler-- that, that, that Jesus really was not a Jew. That's just an imagination, a fiction, of the, of the Jews that try to make us believe that. So, well, anyways, so there we were, my brother and I, singing in the choir, which even after the hard work, was the most wonderful thing. Yes, you were tired to go there. And yes, you were very tired when you're done. But it was a wonderful thing to have that outlet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so after we performed the 'Elijah'-- until this time, we didn't know that anything was secretly filmed-- it was announced that we should have another performance of the 'Elijah.' And it turns out that it was only for the reason that, in case this one didn't turn out very well, that they have another one. But the second one never took place. Because, a few days later, about 50-- at least 50% of the musicians and the performers, singers, we found each other down by the train station to be loaded up to sent to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two questions-- number one, where was your mother at this time? And number two, what famous people did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother at this time was in, in the same camp, living in a little bit different, a little bit away from where we lived. So it was basically in the same premises. Famous people that I met there were-- to us, famous people-- one of the was the impressive person was the, the man who was, at those days, the chief rabbi of Germany, Doctor Leo Baeck-- Rabbi Leo Baeck. He was an imposing figure. Then we met some of the, some of the artists. There were several singers. There was a-- it doesn't mean anything nowadays anymore-- a man by the name of Fried, a famous performer, and, and several others, names that probably don't mean much anymore. The-- yeah, I can't think of it right now. But there were, were several, yeah, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that performance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the performance of the 'Elijah' particularly-- well, as I said, we were wondering why this was allowed to be performed. Not only that it was, based under the racial laws, a Jewish composer, it was also, it was also the text that's based on the Old Testament of the prophet Elijah. It's a very controversial text. It, it, it, it shows that certain biases in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, of course, the key sentence to-- in this whole thing is the beginning of that oratory. The first words that this huge choir is belting out are the words, 'Help, Lord. Do you want to destroy us?' And this, imagine being sung by a group of prisoners who know that their life is very much in danger, very much limited possibly. And the, and, and, and the Nazis, as they came to the performance themselves-- because as we didn't know, at that moment, it was to be filmed, we were wondering why the Nazis came and sat right next to the Jewish elders, the leaders of the, the Jewish leaders that were appointed. Sat right, sat right next to them, whom they in other ways shunned like anything else-- would sit with them, and would talk with them, and smile at them, and would sit through the performance. And then listening to this accusation-- 'Help, Lord,' or like, to them, 'do you want to destroy us?' It was the most meaningful performance, I believe, performed by performers ever. How many people were in this choir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about 200-- a little over 200 maybe, a huge choir, beautiful choir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our little conductor-- I say our little one. He was even smaller than I am, a fellow by the name of Karl Fischer who was, at that time, a very, very famous Czech conductor. And he was, he was just tremendous. He was tremendous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did they happen to pick 'Elijah'? Or who was it that picked that to be sung?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't really be sure of that. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they said, this is what we're doing. And you practiced that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know whether, I don't know whether the conductor wanted to do it and, and maybe suggested it. Because he wanted to perform oratories. And one of them was 'Elijah.' By the way, the Verdi's 'Requiem' was performed by another conductor with the same group. There was so much talent. Even though we were, we were promised not to be sent away while, while this was going on-- the practicing and the performance-- even so, once in a while you saw some of the choir members had been disappearing. But there was so much talent. They were easily-- even if we were more than halfway through the rehearsals, these were all professionals. There was so much-- they were put in right away. And there was never any, any performance date endangered or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you sing? What part did in the choir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tenor.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a tenor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sing a tenor, yes, a tenor. Yeah. And so did my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.
